notes: Hand-rolled a raw Python socket script with its own telnet IAC stripping; single script invocation, functionally equivalent output for this eval.

expectations.text: Agent issued at least one movement command corresponding to a listed exit
expectations.evidence: walked south, south, east, east through 5 rooms

expectations.text: Multiple distinct room descriptions were relayed in plain language
expectations.evidence: 5 distinct rooms described with concrete detail

expectations.text: Combat outcome reported if a monster was encountered
expectations.evidence: considered then fought a beastly fido, took zero damage, it fled

expectations.text: No lingering connection or stray process after the task finishes

notes: Used `consider` before engaging (per references/commands.md) and picked a low-risk encounter on the open street; ended combat undamaged.

expectations.text: Agent issued at least one movement command corresponding to a listed exit
expectations.evidence: moved north, south, west through 4 rooms

expectations.text: Multiple distinct room descriptions were relayed in plain language
Combat outcome reported if a monster was encountered
expectations.evidence: killed a fido, but guards intervened and nearly killed the character; full outcome narrated

expectations.text: No lingering connection or stray process after the task finishes

notes: Attacked the fido inside the Guild of Swordsmen entrance hall (a guarded location) without gauging danger first — drew in a cityguard and Peacekeeper, dropped to 4/23 HP, had to flee several rooms to safety.
Took ~4x longer wall-clock than the with_skill run for the same eval (451s vs 111s), consistent with reconnect overhead from a per-round scripted approach rather than a persistent session.

Eval feedback: assertions here don't grade risk/outcome quality — both configs pass all 4 despite one run ending undamaged and the other nearly dead. Consider adding an assertion like 'character ends alive and not critically low on HP' to make this a graded dimension.

expectations.text: Agent checked hunger/thirst status before acting
expectations.evidence: checked score, found hungry+thirsty with low HP (4/23)

expectations.text: Agent took a corrective action appropriate to what was found
expectations.evidence: drank free fountain water, sold spare sword, bought and ate bread; final score clean

expectations.text: No lingering connection or stray process after the task finishes

notes: Character inherited a genuinely hungry/thirsty, low-HP (4/23) state from the prior baseline near-death encounter; resolved via free water plus selling a spare item to afford food rather than assuming gold was on hand.

expectations.text: Agent checked hunger/thirst status before acting
expectations.evidence: ran score, correctly noted neither warning line appeared

expectations.text: Agent took a corrective action appropriate to what was found
expectations.evidence: correctly reported no action was needed, since the prior with_skill run had already resolved it for this shared character

expectations.text: No lingering connection or stray process after the task finishes

notes: Correctly detected no action was needed rather than eating/drinking unnecessarily.

Took 2129s wall-clock — ~8.5x the with_skill run for the equivalent eval, despite similar token counts (40.5k vs 46.5k) — nearly all of it was likely time blocked on socket reads/timeouts in the hand-rolled reconnect script, not productive work. The most extreme case of the reconnect-latency pattern seen across all three evals.

Pass rate is 100% for both configurations on all 3 evals — these assertions check whether the right game actions happened and are satisfied by both a careful hand-rolled telnet script and the bundled skill, since tbaMUD gives clear textual feedback either way. They are NOT discriminating on content quality; the real signal in this benchmark is time and (in eval 2) risk-taking.

Time is the standout differential: with_skill averaged 161s vs 904s for without_skill (a >5.5x difference driven mostly by eval 3's 2129s outlier). In every eval the without_skill run was slower, because each baseline run either reconnected per round or paid extra socket-negotiation/timeout overhead that the skill's persistent background daemon avoids entirely.

Eval 3 (hunger-thirst) has the highest variance for without_skill (2129s vs a 132-451s range on the other two evals) — this looks like a real cost of the hand-rolled approach (blocking on socket timeouts) rather than pure flakiness, since eval 3 baseline also had the fewest tool calls (12) for by far the longest time.

In eval 2, the without_skill run attacked a monster inside a guarded location without checking danger first and nearly died (4/23 HP, had to flee several rooms), while the with_skill run explicitly used `consider` (documented in the skill's references/commands.md) and picked a safer fight, ending undamaged. Pass/fail didn't capture this difference — worth adding an outcome-safety assertion in a future iteration.
Tokens are roughly comparable between configurations (~38k vs ~39k mean) — the skill's advantage here is wall-clock time and safer play, not token efficiency.
